name: Incident/accident reporting
current_process: When an incident or accident occurs in a care home — a fall, medication error, aggressive behaviour, near-miss, or safeguarding concern — the staff member on duty completes a paper incident report form or logs the event in a basic digital system. The form captures date, time, location, individuals involved, witnesses, a narrative description, immediate actions taken, and injury details. The report must be completed within 24 hours. The registered manager then reviews, categorises the incident by severity, and decides on escalation: internally to the provider's head office, and externally to the CQC (via statutory notifications), the local authority safeguarding team, and/or the HSE under RIDDOR if a reportable injury, disease, or dangerous occurrence is involved. Body maps may be completed for visible injuries. The manager also updates the resident's care plan and risk assessments to reflect the incident, and logs the event in an accident book (legally required for workplaces with 10+ employees). Trend analysis — identifying patterns such as recurring falls on a particular shift or in a specific location — is typically done manually using spreadsheets or by flicking through paper logs at governance meetings. Duty of candour requirements mean the resident and/or their family must be informed and the outcome of any investigation shared.
time_spent: Estimated 3–8 hours per week for a registered manager depending on home size and incident volume. Individual incident forms take 20–45 minutes to complete. A 60-bed care home with an average of 10–15 incidents per month (falls being the most common) can expect the manager to spend 4–6 hours per month on form completion alone, plus 2–3 hours on follow-up actions, CQC notifications, RIDDOR reports, and trend analysis. Significant incidents requiring safeguarding referrals or HSE reporting can consume an additional 2–4 hours each. Monthly governance reviews of incident data add a further 1–2 hours.
ai_solution: AI can transform incident reporting from a reactive, paper-heavy process into a proactive, intelligence-driven system: (1) Voice-to-text capture allows staff to dictate incident details immediately at the point of occurrence, with AI structuring the narrative into the correct form fields (date, time, location, type, severity, witnesses, actions). (2) Automatic categorisation and severity scoring using NLP to classify incidents (fall, medication error, behavioural, environmental) and flag high-severity events for immediate escalation. (3) Auto-generation of CQC statutory notifications and RIDDOR reports from incident data, pre-populated and ready for manager review and submission. (4) Pattern recognition and predictive analytics — AI analyses incident data over time to identify trends (e.g., falls clustering on night shifts, in specific rooms, or among residents on particular medications), enabling preventive interventions before harm occurs. (5) Automatic updates to the resident's care plan and risk assessment triggered by the incident type. (6) Dashboard summaries for governance meetings, replacing manual spreadsheet analysis. Audracare already offers AI-powered incident tracking with pattern identification in UK care homes.
buyer_language: "Every time someone has a fall or a near-miss, I'm filling out forms, notifying CQC, updating care plans, and trying to spot patterns in a pile of paper. I spend more time writing about incidents than preventing them."
outcome_statement: Incident reports completed in minutes instead of hours — with automatic categorisation, CQC notifications pre-drafted, and AI-powered trend analysis that identifies risks before they become serious harm.
regulatory_alignment: Maps directly to CQC Quality Statement: 'Learning culture' under the Safe key question — 'We have a proactive and positive culture of safety based on openness and honesty, in which concerns about safety are listened to, safety events are investigated and reported thoroughly, and lessons are learned to continually identify and embed good practices.' Also maps to 'Safeguarding' (Safe), 'Involving people to manage risks' (Safe), and 'Governance, management and sustainability' (Well-led). Underpinned by CQC Regulation 12 (Safe care and treatment) and Regulation 20 (Duty of candour). RIDDOR 2013 governs HSE reporting obligations.
existing_tech_gap: Current care management platforms (Nourish, Person Centred Software, Log my Care, CareVision) offer digital incident recording forms and some basic reporting dashboards, but they function primarily as structured data entry — staff still manually type narratives, select categories, and generate reports. Audracare offers AI-powered incident tracking with pattern analysis. However, no mainstream UK platform offers voice-to-text incident capture, automatic severity classification via NLP, auto-generated CQC notifications, or predictive analytics that flag emerging risk patterns before incidents escalate. AI adds unique value through real-time trend detection, predictive risk flagging, and eliminating the documentation bottleneck at point of care.
implementation_complexity: Plug-and-play for basic AI-assisted form completion and categorisation. Requires integration for auto-populating CQC notification portals and linking incident data to care plan updates in existing systems (Nourish, PCS). Predictive analytics requires a sufficient volume of historical incident data (typically 6–12 months) to train pattern recognition models.
proof_of_concept_availability: Partial. Audracare offers a working AI-powered incident tracking system in the UK market with pattern identification capabilities. NHS England has rolled out Cera's AI tool nationally, which includes automated incident flagging. The Vatix platform offers digital incident management for care homes. No fully integrated UK solution combines voice-to-text capture, NLP categorisation, auto-generated CQC notifications, and predictive analytics in a single product yet.
